EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tincup fire began July 30th due to a lightning strike and burned the entire month of August.  It was located approximately 30 miles northeast of Soda Springs.  It was allowed to burn naturally and grew to 264 acres by the end of August.  It reduced heavy dead and down fuel loading that was above historic range to to past fire suppression.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tincup fire began July 30th due to a lightning strike and burned the entire month of August.  It was located approximately 30 miles northeast of Soda Springs.  It was allowed to burn naturally and grew to 264 acres by the end of August.  It reduced heavy dead and down fuel loading that was above historic range to to past fire suppression.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused the 4,889 acre Spud Fire approximately 12 miles southwest of Carey.  It burned grass and brush and threatened no structures.  It began around 5:30 PM MDT on August 1st and was controlled on August 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused the 4,889 acre Spud Fire approximately 12 miles southwest of Carey.  It burned grass and brush and threatened no structures.  It began around 5:30 PM MDT on August 1st and was controlled on August 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service photographed a tornado southwest of Lake Wolcott at 1:55 PM MDT on August 2nd.  It occurred over open land and no damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service photographed a tornado southwest of Lake Wolcott at 1:55 PM MDT on August 2nd.  It occurred over open land and no damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some tree damage occurred 4 miles north of Lago.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three branches were downed from a cottonwood tree 4 miles north of Lago.  The largest was 4 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 9th a mud slide occurred one mile past the east fort of Baker Creek.   Warm Springs Road was impassable from the west of the Hot Springs in Ketchum.  3 to 5 inches of mud covered the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 9th a mud slide occurred one mile past the east fort of Baker Creek.   Warm Springs Road was impassable from the west of the Hot Springs in Ketchum.  3 to 5 inches of mud covered the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Treasureton Fore was started bu farm equipment on August 15th at about 3:30 pm MDT about 13 miles north of Preston near Treasureton .  It grew to 363 acres in rugged terrain covered by grass, brush and juniper.  No structures were threatened and it was contained on August 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Treasureton Fore was started bu farm equipment on August 15th at about 3:30 pm MDT about 13 miles north of Preston near Treasureton .  It grew to 363 acres in rugged terrain covered by grass, brush and juniper.  No structures were threatened and it was contained on August 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire began between 1 and 2 pm MDT due to sparks from a broken drive line on a vehicle and spread to both sides of US Highway 26. Highway 26 closed for a period of time due to the fire near mile marker 300. although strong winds drove the fire and threatened structures it was able to be contained with no damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire began between 1 and 2 pm MDT due to sparks from a broken drive line on a vehicle and spread to both sides of US Highway 26. Highway 26 closed for a period of time due to the fire near mile marker 300. although strong winds drove the fire and threatened structures it was able to be contained with no damages.

CZ_NAME: LOWER HELLS CANYON / SALMON RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures occurred in central Idaho therefore compromising sensitive vegetation such as flowering fruit trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low of 29 degrees was recorded just south of Riggins which is near the threshold for compromising potential blossom drop in some fruit trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet Pacific system brought snow, freezing rain and rain with many changes in type in low elevations of the Snake River Plain during the three days with mostly snow in higher elevations.  Extensive travel problems were caused by the system with school closures on both the 17th and 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought freezing rain on the 17th to the Upper Snake River Plain.  Many private schools closed in Idaho Falls on the 17th with many of the public schools remaining open.   A Jefferson County school bus collided with a semi truck around 7:27 AM MST just north of Osgood on the icy interstate.  Another semi truck crashed south of Idaho Falls near mile post 106.  Multiple slideoffs reported between Blackfoot and Idaho Falls.  Heaviest reported snowfall with the system was 4.0 inches by the Saint Anthony COOP observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet Pacific system brought snow, freezing rain and rain with many changes in type in low elevations of the Snake River Plain during the three days with mostly snow in higher elevations.  Extensive travel problems were caused by the system with school closures on both the 17th and 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 9 to 10 inches of snow in Hailey with the Bellevue COOP observer reporting 8.3 inches.  The Picabo COOP observer had 5.0 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ketchum Ranger Station COOP observer reported 13.5 inches of snow from the 3 day winter storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Upper Snake River Highlands with snow on rain causing horrible travel in the Snake River Plain and Marsh and Arbon Highlands with one fatality reported on the roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Island Park COOP observer reported 14.4 inches of snow with the White Elephant SNOTEL reporting 14.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier and snowboarder were buried for 25 minutes on the 20th.  The two were carried 100 to 150 yards in an avalanche they triggered in the Warm Springs Creek drainage riding in the out-of-bounds area of Bald Mountain.  IT was 100 feet wide near 7,800 feet elevation and they were able to dig out and did not suffer serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier and snowboarder were buried for 25 minutes on the 20th.  The two were carried 100 to 150 yards in an avalanche they triggered in the Warm Springs Creek drainage riding in the out-of-bounds area of Bald Mountain.  IT was 100 feet wide near 7,800 feet elevation and they were able to dig out and did not suffer serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow mobiler died in an avalanche that occurred around 6 pm on the 25th near Palisades Lake in Bonneville County near the Wyoming border.  A 35 year old male died din the avalanche that occurred near Palisades Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler died in an avalanche that occurred around 6 pm on the 25th near Palisades Lake in Bonneville County near the Wyoming border. A 35 year old male died din the avalanche that occurred near Palisades Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was partially responsible for a train ans SUV crash on US 20 near mile post 343 south of Saint Anthony.   The vehicle failed to yield to a train.   Several other vehicles went off the road.   The accident caused the road to be backed up for miles.   Idaho State Police were forced to divert traffic.  No injuries occurred due to the accident.  The accident with the train and SUV occurred around noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was partially responsible for a train ans SUV crash on US 20 near mile post 343 south of Saint Anthony.   The vehicle failed to yield to a train.   Several other vehicles went off the road.   The accident caused the road to be backed up for miles.   Idaho State Police were forced to divert traffic.  No injuries occurred due to the accident.  The accident with the train and SUV occurred around noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm affected southeast Idaho for several days with heavy snow and strong winds creating closed roads, multiple traffic accidents and many school closings on January 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm produced heavy snowfall and very strong winds making travel extremely hazardous.  A semi-truck and snow plow got in an accident on interstate 15 six miles south of Dubois on the 7th.   Clark County School District 161 closed on January 7th due to the dangerous travel conditions.   The COOP observer 6 miles north of Small recorded 6.3 inches of snow from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm affected southeast Idaho for several days with heavy snow and strong winds creating closed roads, multiple traffic accidents and many school closings on January 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall and very strong winds caused extremely difficult travel in the Upper Snake River Plain .  The following schools were closed on January 7th due to road conditions caused by the snow and wind:  Madison School District 321, Fremont School District 215, Sugar-Salem Joint School District 322, Ririe School District 252.  Snow amounts were not extraordinarily heavy with COOP observers reporting 3 inches in Rigby and 4.5 inches in Hamer.  US Highway 26 was closed from Ririe to Swan Valley due to the snow and wind on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm affected southeast Idaho for several days with heavy snow and strong winds creating closed roads, multiple traffic accidents and many school closings on January 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy snow fell in the Wood River Foothills.   Amounts measured were:  16 inches 4 miles northwest of Bellevue, 14 inches 2 miles north-northwest of Hailey, 10.3 inches at the Bellevue COOP station, 11 inches 3 miles north of Hailey, and 9 inches at the Picabo COOP station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused ice to develop on roadways in the Lower Snake River Plain and 2 inches of snow in the Upper Snake River Plain caused traffic issues as well.   Interstate 86 east bound was closed for two hours east of American Falls and Highway 20 east bound was shut down for over an hour between Idaho Falls and Ucon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell on the morning of the 10th from American Falls through the Pocatello area causing a glaze of ice to form on the roads.   A traffic accident due to the icy roads closed interstate 86 east bound for over two hours east of American Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused ice to develop on roadways in the Lower Snake River Plain and 2 inches of snow in the Upper Snake River Plain caused traffic issues as well.   Interstate 86 east bound was closed for two hours east of American Falls and Highway 20 east bound was shut down for over an hour between Idaho Falls and Ucon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 20 was closed east bound from Idaho Falls to Ucon due to a  trailer flipping on a snow covered road.   Several slide offs and accidents were reported in the Idaho Falls area.  Although snowfall was not heavy, 2 to 2.5 inches of snow was reported in the Idaho Falls area and caused extensive traffic problems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some property and farmland flooding occurred south of Moore in Butte County on January 9th along the Big Lost River.  A land owner rescued his cattle from the flooding with none lost due to the flood.  Hay storage was also safely relocated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some property and farmland flooding occurred south of Moore in Butte County on January 9th along the Big Lost River. A land owner rescued his cattle from the flooding with none lost due to the flood. Hay storage was also safely relocated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet Pacific system brought snow, freezing rain and rain with many changes in type in low elevations of the Snake River Plain during the three days with mostly snow in higher elevations.  Extensive travel problems were caused by the system with school closures on both the 17th and 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long drawn out system brought a variety of weather to the Lower Snake River Plain.   Freezing rain caused schools to close on the 17th for Blackfoot School District 55 and Snake River School District 52.  Many private schools also closed in Blackfoot and Bingham County on the 17th.  Precipitation was rain for much of the 17th then changed to snow during the overnight and morning period of the 17th and 18th.   A burst of heavy snow brought 2 to 4 inches on the morning of the 18th to the Pocatello and Chubbuck and Fort Hall areas and the heavy snow on rain that froze caused very icy roads and Pocatello/Chubbuck School District 25 closed on the 18th.   Many other schools in Pocatello and Fort Hall also closed due to the conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon of January 8th through the morning of the 9th a slow moving moist warm front brought heavy snow to much of the northern Idaho Panhandle.  A tongue of warm air behind the warm front reached well into the Idaho Panhandle during this event with minor accumulations mixed with freezing rain in the Coeur D'Alene area and rain |and minor snow accumulations to the south. Only locally heavy mountain accumulations of snow were noted on the higher peaks of the central Idaho Panhandle. The warm front stalled between Coeur D'Alene and Sandpoint with the northern Idaho panhandle receiving heavy snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Rathdrum reported freezing rain on top of 1.5 inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and through the night of January 22nd a cold low pressure system tracked through the region bringing widespread heavy snow to the mountains and valleys of north Idaho. Snow tapered off in the valleys on the morning of the 23rd but upslope snow showers in the wake of the low pressure continued to feed into the mountains of the panhandle with further accumulations through the day of January 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Mullan recorded 5.7 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 9 miles south of Elmira recorded 5.5 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the evening of January 6th through the early morning of January 7th an area of persistent snow showers developed over the north Idaho Panhandle in an unstable air mass behind a cold front. Numerous reports of heavy snow accumulations were received from locations between Sandpoint and Bayview underneath these showers as well as from mountain locations which received sustained moist upslope flow in this cold post frontal air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Schweitzer Mountain remote weather station recorded 8.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Upper Snake River Highlands with snow on rain causing horrible travel in the Snake River Plain and Marsh and Arbon Highlands with one fatality reported on the roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several car accidents occurred on the morning of the 21st as a burst of snow after a cold frontal passage froze roads that had been wet from extensive rainfall.   A vehicle in Pocatello crashed into a fire truck and both the car and fire truck sustained moderate damage.   Another vehicle slid into the retention pond on the southbound Clark Street exit of interstate 15.  Pocatello police responded to 8 accidents on the morning of the 21st.  1 to 3 inches of snow fell in the Pocatello area on the morning of the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding returned south of Moore due to ice jamming of the Big Lost River on the 14th.  Only farm land was flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding returned south of Moore due to ice jamming of the Big Lost River on the 14th.  Only farm land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Upper Snake River Highlands with snow on rain causing horrible travel in the Snake River Plain and Marsh and Arbon Highlands with one fatality reported on the roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 3 inches of snow over wet roads that froze caused a fatal traffic accident on US Highway 30 near mile post 361 in McCammon.   The crash occurred at 10 am.  A driver lost control and crossed the center of the roadwy on snow covered roads causing a head-on collision.   A 74 year old man was killed in the accident which closed the road for 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon of January 8th through the morning of the 9th a slow moving moist warm front brought heavy snow to much of the northern Idaho Panhandle.  A tongue of warm air behind the warm front reached well into the Idaho Panhandle during this event with minor accumulations mixed with freezing rain in the Coeur D'Alene area and rain |and minor snow accumulations to the south. Only locally heavy mountain accumulations of snow were noted on the higher peaks of the central Idaho Panhandle. The warm front stalled between Coeur D'Alene and Sandpoint with the northern Idaho panhandle receiving heavy snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Naples reported 7.0 inches of new snow accumulation with this storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pebble Creek ski area mid mountain measuring site recorded the greatest seasonal snowfall of 339 inches through March 15th breaking the record of 330 inches set in 2016-2017.    This amount was recorded from December 1st through March 15th.   The ski resort is located south of the city of Pocatello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of March 11th and lasting through the morning of March 12th a moist slow moving cold front passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho. The northern reaches of the Idaho Panhandle received heavy snow accumulations from this storm system while locations farther south generally received more moderate 2 to 4 inch amounts with locally higher amounts in the mountains and valleys of the central Idaho Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Prichard recorded 9.1 inches of new snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain combined with accumulating very small hail caused many traffic accidents on interstates 15 and 86 from Fort Hall to Pocatello and from Pocatello to American Falls.  Some injuries occurred and traffic was backed up for several miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although listed as sleet this was a heavy rain and small hail event.   It was listed as sleet to ensure a zone forecast area rather than counties as it covered multiple counties.  ||Very heavy rain combined with accumulating very small hail caused many traffic accidents on interstates 15 and 86 from Fort Hall to Pocatello and from Pocatello to American Falls.  Some injuries occurred and traffic was backed up for several miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed by Blaine County Road and Bridge on April 8th in Hailey due to heavy snow melt.  They were Parker Gulch, Rock Creek Rd, W. Glendale Rd, Road Canyon, Meacham Lane, and Dry Creek Rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed by Blaine County Road and Bridge on April 8th in Hailey due to heavy snow melt.  They were Parker Gulch, Rock Creek Rd, W. Glendale Rd, Road Canyon, Meacham Lane, and Dry Creek Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding began April 21st on War Eagle Drive in Hailey.   Through access through the Della View neighborhood was shut down in western Hailey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding began April 21st on War Eagle Drive in Hailey. Through access through the Della View neighborhood was shut down in western Hailey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 60 mph along interstate 15 north of Idaho Falls and Roberts on April 27th.   Interstate 15 shut down due to the wind and blowing dust in a 16 mile stretch between Idaho Falls and Roberts.   The wind gusted to 55 mph at the Idaho Falls airport ASOS.   The interstate closed at around 5 pm MDT.   A semi tractor trailer rolled on interstate 4 miles north of Roberts possibly due to the high winds at around 3:30 pm MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 60 mph along interstate 15 north of Idaho Falls and Roberts on April 27th.   Interstate 15 shut down due to the wind and blowing dust in a 16 mile stretch between Idaho Falls and Roberts.   The wind gusted to 55 mph at the Idaho Falls airport ASOS.   The interstate closed at around 5 pm MDT.   A semi tractor trailer rolled on interstate 4 miles north of Roberts possibly due to the high winds at around 3:30 pm MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rainfall combined with snow melt contributed to flooding, mud-slides and road wash-outs and closures. More specifically, the South Fork of the Clearwater River saw an all-time record crest of 10.79 feet on the morning of April 9 which contributed to the town of Stites being flooded. Damages to Idaho County infrastructure from this event have totaled to around 6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork of the Clearwater River at Stites, crested at a record level of 10.79 feet on the morning of April 9. This led to flooding of 3 to 5 homes along the west side of the river along North River Road.  A torrent of fast-moving water developed in an unnamed gulch located east of Stites along Stites Grade Road and this led to the closure of Idaho Highway 13 and the flooding of Stites. Approximately 30 to 50 homes were affected by the flooding in the town of Stites with many wells contaminated and the National Guard rescued two people who were trapped in their home. Very large mud-slides caused US-12 to close between Orofino and Kamiah from April 9 to April 13. A home in Syringa was flooded by a debris flow and US-12 between Kooskia and Syringa experienced erosion and water running on the road leading to the closure of Highway 12. Clear Creek, located approximately 2 miles southeast of Kooskia, washed out two private bridges, flooded approximately 5 homes, destroyed a trailer home, and damaged county road Clear Creek Road. The Nez Perce U.S. Forest Service closed Smith Creek Road due to flooding of the Little Smith Creek causing a washout. Numerous other bridges, culverts and roads were washed out across Idaho County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures occurred in central Idaho therefore compromising sensitive vegetation such as flowering fruit trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low of 28 degrees was recorded in Grangeville which is right at the threshold for compromising potential blossom drop in some fruit trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in association with a strong upper low moving through Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 MPH thunderstorm wind gust was reported 1 mile west of Glenns Ferry by a resident with a home weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Atmospheric River brought a persistent stream of moisture off the Pacific Ocean to West Central Idaho April 6-10.  Rainfall totals of 2 to 5 inches were measured at many weather stations while snow melt added 1 to 3 inches of melt-water to the runoff. Main stem river flooding, flash flooding, and small stream flooding occurred during the event with record or near record crests observed on a number of waterways. In addition, debris flows on past years wildfire burned areas, rock slides, and mudslides occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington County emergency manager reported moderate flooding near the U.S. Highway 95 bridge in Weiser. The flooding caused extensive damage and the closure of the highway for 4 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell in Fremont County during the morning of the 15th with 2 to 4 inches.  The snow caused very dangerous road conditions.   A semi truck rolled off US Highway 20 near Elk Creek Station around milepost 388.   Other crashes occurred north of Ashton and near Mack's Inn between 8 and 9 am MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell in Fremont County during the morning of the 15th with 2 to 4 inches. The snow caused very dangerous road conditions. A semi truck rolled off US Highway 20 near Elk Creek Station around milepost 388. Other crashes occurred north of Ashton and near Mack's Inn between 8 and 9 am MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An  upper level trough situated over Northern Nevada provided for unstable conditions which aided in strong thunderstorm development, including a funnel cloud across Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported by social media near Cow Creek in Owyhee County. The location is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on April 6th and continuing through April 10th a series of Pacific atmospheric rivers were directed into Oregon, southeast Washington and central Idaho saturating the ground with heavy rainfall. During this 4 day period the COOP station at Kamiah reported 1.86 inches of rain, Elk River reported 2.94 inches of rain, Moscow 3.44 inches, Nez Perce 2.05 inches, Potlatch 2.46 inches, Dworshack Fish Hatchery 3.08 inches , the Lewiston Airport ASOS 0.93 inches and the ASOS at Pullman, WA recording 2.88 inches of rain. By the 9th of April widespread areal flooding issues from swollen streams and rivers as well as mud slides were noted around the region which persisted until the 11th when heavy rain ceased and rivers and streams began to recede.||Kamiah, ID suffered minor flooding of businesses, utilities and park land from the Clearwater River on the eastern boundary of the city and and the breaching of a levee on Lawyer Creek on the southern edge of the city. Lapwai Creek also overflowed it's banks threatening homes and flooding bottom lands and portions of State Highway 95 between Lapwai and Culdesac. Tribal officials of the Nez Perce reservation, which includes most of this region, declared a State of Emergency in response to this flooding event.  ||In the city of Moscow after receiving 1.47 inches of rain on April 9th, Paradise Creek rose to it's highest flood level on record causing extensive minor flooding of portions downtown Moscow. This flooding was aggravated by a Flash Flood running through residential neighborhoods and business districts laying in in gullies and ravines draining the saturated hills to the north of the city ||The Palouse River also reached flood stage with inundation of bottom lands and fields along the river with some minor flooding of agricultural out buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mud slides were reported over Highway 12 just west of Kamiah on the morning of April 9th. One of the slides completely covered the road and caused the temporary closure of this primary road through the region while crews cleared the debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water in the Clearwater River augmented by a levee break along Lawyer Creek  produced extensive flooding near the confluence of these two streams on the eastern edge of the city of Kamiah. The flood waters were reported to be waist deep in the Kamiah Riverfront Park. Flood waters also partially inundated a lumber yard, undermined a railroad grade and trestle crossing Lawyer Creek and high water prohibited access to the city's water plant. The levee was temporarily repaired by the public works department as the creek receded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the afternoon and evening of April 9th caused Paradise Creek running through Moscow to rapidly rise and inundate lower elevation portions of the city along the creek.  In addition heavy rain in the hills north of Moscow caused flash flooding in the northeastern areas of the city as runoff drained through valleys and ravines along North Polk Street and into the East D street neighborhood, then through downtown before  draining into Paradise Creek.  In some areas flood waters were reported to be 3 feet deep with numerous roads, intersections and underpasses impassable. Most of the reported damage involved basement and first floor flooding of buildings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on Paradise Creek in Moscow recorded a sharp rise to above the Flood Stage of 9.2 feet at 2:45 PM PDT on April 9th. The creek continued to rise passing through the Moderate Flood Stage of 10.0 feet at 7:00 PM. The creek continued to rise and crested at 11.4 feet at 7:15 PM which is a record high crest beating the previous record crest of 11.3 feet set in 1996. The creek then quickly receded passing below the moderate Flood Stage at 8:00 PM and then below Flood Stage at 9:15 PM PDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the Palouse River near Potlatch recorded a rise to the Flood Stage of 15.0 feet at 8:30 PM PDT on April 9th. The river continued to rise and pass through the Moderate Flood Stage of 16.0 feet at 10:45 pm on the 9th. The river crested just under the Major Flood Stage of 17.0 feet at 5:00 am on the 10th and then began receding. The river passed down through the Moderate Flood Stage at 3:00 PM on the 10th and then receded below the Flood Stage at 9:15 PM PDT on April 10th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down 2 miles south of Riverside nd moved north for 1.8 miles crossing the Snake River before dissipating just south os W 100 S (Thomas Road) and between S 275 W and S 670 W just south of Riverside.  Damage consisted of a mix of several young and mature cottonwood trees that were wither snapped or uprooted on both private and BLM land . One downed live tree measured a foot in diameter.  Another much larger downed tree was found with a diameter close to two feet.  Minor damage was reported to a greenhouse.  Maximum wind speeds were estimated at 80 to 85 mph with path width of 50-100 yards.  It was on the ground for 1.8 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved up from the south during the day on February 10th with more heavy snow over the central Idaho Panhandle, then a following cold front created a period of intense snow fall as it passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho from west to east during the night of February 10th, with upslope snow showers in the wake of this front continuing to drive into the Idaho Panhandle mountains during the morning of the 11th. Heavy snow fell over the Idaho Panhandle mainly south of Sandpoint with moderate accumulations in the far northern panhandle. This was the second of a series of disruptive winter storms to impact the region during the second week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Wallace recorded 12.4 inches of snow accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Kellogg recorded 7.0 inches of new snow from this storm.

Snow developed along this front and deposited a narrow corridor of heavy snow across far southeast Washington and the central and southern Idaho Panhandle, with generally 1 to 3 inches across the southern Columbia Basin in Washington and the northern Idaho Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter at Winchester reported 6.5 inches of new snow accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and blowing snow brought significant travel impacts across the Camas Prairie. The Idaho Department of Transportation urged travelers to only travel if absolutely necessary on Highway 95 due to impacts from snow and blowing snow. Multiple accidents, including a jackknifed semi truck were reported during the morning of the 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of winter storms to strike the region during the second week of February developed over north Idaho as a moist warm front during the afternoon and evening hours of February 11th. Steady light to moderate snow fell through February 12th and continued into February 13th as the warm front stalled thus providing a focusing mechanism for a continuous Pacific moisture feed into the region. This resulted in widespread heavy snow accumulations over most of north Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Moscow reported 8.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first winter storm in what would become a repeating pattern of winter storms during the second week of February developed as an arctic cold front descended out of British Columbia and undercut a mid level warm front appending off of a surface low pressure near the Washington coast. The interaction of these air masses produced moderate to locally heavy snow accumulations in the Idaho Panhandle during the day and evening of February 8th.||During and after the snow fall period the arctic intrusion produced strong gusty northerly and northeasterly winds down the Purcell Trench of north Idaho from Bonners Ferry to Coeur D'Alene producing local blizzard conditions in blowing and drifting snow with scattered power outages and fallen trees during the day and into the evening of February 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 5 miles north of Spirit Lake reported 4.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandpoint Airport ASOS station reported northerly winds of 35 to 40 mph with gusts as high as 46 mph during the overnight hours of February 8th through the morning of the 9th, producing areas of blowing and drifting snow down the Purcell Trench of north Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved up from the south during the day on February 10th with more heavy snow over the central Idaho Panhandle, then a following cold front created a period of intense snow fall as it passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho from west to east during the night of February 10th, with upslope snow showers in the wake of this front continuing to drive into the Idaho Panhandle mountains during the morning of the 11th. Heavy snow fell over the Idaho Panhandle mainly south of Sandpoint with moderate accumulations in the far northern panhandle. This was the second of a series of disruptive winter storms to impact the region during the second week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Coeur D'Alene recorded 7.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning the afternoon of February 14th and continuing through the night into the morning of the 15th a moist warm front moved south to north through the Idaho Panhandle. Initial light snow south of interstate 90 changed to rain with the warm front passage during the evening hours with light snow accumulations before this change-over. The warm air tongue did not penetrate the northern panhandle during the night with heavy snow accumulations noted north of Interstate 90 from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer near Naples recorded 6.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first winter storm in what would become a repeating pattern of winter storms during the second week of February developed as an arctic cold front descended out of British Columbia and undercut a mid level warm front appending off of a surface low pressure near the Washington coast. The interaction of these air masses produced moderate to locally heavy snow accumulations in the Idaho Panhandle during the day and evening of February 8th.||During and after the snow fall period the arctic intrusion produced strong gusty northerly and northeasterly winds down the Purcell Trench of north Idaho from Bonners Ferry to Coeur D'Alene producing local blizzard conditions in blowing and drifting snow with scattered power outages and fallen trees during the day and into the evening of February 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Post Falls reported 6.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved up from the south during the day on February 10th with more heavy snow over the central Idaho Panhandle, then a following cold front created a period of intense snow fall as it passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho from west to east during the night of February 10th, with upslope snow showers in the wake of this front continuing to drive into the Idaho Panhandle mountains during the morning of the 11th. Heavy snow fell over the Idaho Panhandle mainly south of Sandpoint with moderate accumulations in the far northern panhandle. This was the second of a series of disruptive winter storms to impact the region during the second week of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 6 miles northeast of Lapwai recorded 6.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

Several large trees and branches came down, damaging vehicles and blocking local roads briefly. Power outages were also noted.

Two miles northwest of Idaho Falls a large tree blew onto a car at the Idaho Falls Activity Center at 4:30 pm MDT.    AT 5 pm MDT a small tree was downed onto a vehicle at E Tawzer Way near E Barton Lane 2 miles south southeast of Ammon.   A large tree and Power lines were down on S 5th W near Mesa Falls Apartments in Rexburg at 6:27 pm MDT.  The road was closed by fire and law enforcement.   A large pine tree approximately 3 feet in diameter was uprooted at Pinecrest Gold Course in Idaho Falls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly 9,500 grass and brush wildfire was started by lightning on July 6th at 5:30 MDT.  No structures were threatened and no evacuations were required.  The fire was controlled on July 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused an 8,272 acre fire 7 miles north of Raft River on BLM property.  It was a grass and brush fire that began on July 6th at around 3 pm MDT.  It was controlled on July 8th with no structures threatened and no evacuations required.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Shady wildfire began with a lightning strike on July 10th and burned over 3,600 acres by the end of July in northwestern Custer County.  The fire burned subalpine fir and lodgepole pine.   Many closures occurred including Sheep Mountain Road beginning at the junction of FS Road 40008 at Seafoam Guard Station ending at road 40172.  Forest trail 4005 was closed (Sheep Creek Trail).   Duffield Trail, and Bernard Creek Trail were also closed.  The fire was a semi-contained fire and continued burning through August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shady wildfire began with a lightning strike on July 10th and burned over 3,600 acres by the end of July in northwestern Custer County. Forest trail 4005 was closed (Sheep Creek Trail).   Duffield Trail, and Bernard Creek Trail were also closed.  The fire was a semi-contained fire and continued burning through August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The twenty mile fire began on July 12th at around 8:30 pm MDT just over 20 miles west of Idaho Falls.   It was caused by lightning and burned around 200 acres of grash and brush.  No structures were threatened with no closures or evacuations.  It was controlled by July 14th.   Rain on the 13th helped with containment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sheep wildfire began on July 22nd northeast of the Material Fuels Complex on the Idaho National Laboratory Complex.   It was caused by lightning and by the afternoon of July 23rd had spread to near 90,000 acres all on the complex.    By the morning of the 24th it was estimated at 110,000 acres.  Strong southwest winds Tuesday afternoon caused rapid spread of the fire.  Some work operations at the Complex were affected with some employees not reporting for work the 23rd and 24th.   Full operations resumed by the 25th.   No significant damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave trough passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho with cold and unstable conditions promoting localized heavy snow showers. During the late evening of October 8th and into the early morning hours of October 9th persistent but localized snow showers formed over the Spokane area of eastern Washington and extended into the Coeur D'Alene area of north Idaho. Three to 5 inches of snow accumulation was reported from this area with the highest local accumulation reported as 7.7 inches. |Across the remainder of north Idaho generally 1 to 2 inches was reported with local reports of up to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late October intrusion of arctic air also brought snow and hazardous travel conditions to southeast Idaho.   Some roads closed as well as School District 25 in Pocatello as snow combined with strong winds and very cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on the 28th ahead of an arctic cold front and roads froze quickly on the evening of the 28th.   2 to 4 inches fell in the Pocatello/Chubbuck region through the event and impacts were significant.   Interstate 86 eastbound was shut down from the Yellowstone Ave exit east to the interstate 15 interchange.   40 to 50 vehicles were stranded or off the road.   Multiple vehicles off the road along interstate 15 as well just north of the interstate 86 interchange.   Multiple tractor trailers also stuck on interstate 15 south of the interchange.   This occurred between 9 and 10 pm on the 28th.    The 20 to 35 mph winds caused snow drifts up to 20 inches on some roads in Blackfoot.  A tractor trailer overturned on interstate 15 in Blackfoot a couple of miles south of exit 89.   The Pocatello/Chubbuck School District #25 closed schools on the 29th due to the hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late October intrusion of arctic air also brought snow and hazardous travel conditions to southeast Idaho.   Some roads closed as well as School District 25 in Pocatello as snow combined with strong winds and very cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although only 1 to 3 inches of snow fell, strong northerly winds caused extensive blowing and drifting snow and travel impacts.   US Highway 20 was closed for one hour on the morning of the 29th from Argon National Engineering Lab Road at the INL site to Croft Road west of Idaho Falls due to the drifting snow and multiple slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 12th at about 9:15 pm lightning hit a chimney at the ARTitorium in downtown Idaho Falls.   The Destinations Inn which shares a building with the ARTitorium was evacuated by all 24 guests until fire fighters could inspect the building.   One vehicle was hit by a brick from the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On July 12th at about 9:15 pm lightning hit a chimney at the ARTitorium in downtown Idaho Falls.   The Destinations Inn which shares a building with the ARTitorium was evacuated by all 24 guests until fire fighters could inspect the building.   One vehicle was hit by a brick from the chimney. Damage amounts were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought widespread severe thunderstorm activity to southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 30 large hardwood trees either uprooted or snapped due to a likely microburst.  One tree caused moderate damage to a RV, while another destroyed a pickup truck parked nearby.  Another tree fell onto a baseball field fence.  An open ended trailer rolled one turn on it's side significantly damaging it.   A nearby heavy metal picnic table was flipped and shingle damage noted on a marina facilities building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day moist pattern with snow levels generally around 4500 feet brought wintry impacts to mountain passes and back country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SNOTEL station at Crater Meadows reported 17 of snow. Mountain passes like Lolo and Lookout, received approximately 4 of snow, but due to warm ground conditions,  accumulations melted rapidly. Lost Trail Pass measured around 7 of snow. One car was reported abandoned by Clearwater County dispatch during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 5th thunderstorms formed over northeast Oregon and moved  into extreme southeast Washington and the southern Idaho Panhandle. Scattered reports of trees down and power outages were received from these areas before these storms dissipated later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local newspaper reported a tree blown down in Lewiston due to a thunderstorm wind gust. No damage was reported. Time estimated from radar data. The local utility company reported 2400 customers without power in the Lewiston and Clarkston area as a result of thunderstorm winds bringing down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 5th thunderstorms formed over northeast Oregon and moved  into extreme southeast Washington and the southern Idaho Panhandle. Scattered reports of trees down and power outages were received from these areas before these storms dissipated later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter near Sanders reported multiple trees snapped and uprooted in nearby yards and wooded areas due to thunderstorm wind gusts. The trees were 12 to 20 inches in diameter. No structure damage beyond a downed power line was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 23rd, a strong upper level trough triggered thunderstorms in a moist and unstable air mass over northeast Oregon. These storms moved northeastward into eastern Washington and north Idaho forming into a line of thunderstorms featuring severe wind gusts as they passed through the region. Numerous reports of 60 mph wind gusts, downed trees and power outages were received from northeast Washington and north Idaho during the late evening hours as this line swept through the region. Utility companies reported over 30,000 customers around the region without power in the wake of these storms. One person was injured and had to be rescued when he was pinned under a fallen tree near Bonners Ferry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported multiple downed trees blocking Idaho State Route 3 west of Rose Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 71 mph wind gust occurred at the Pocatello Regional airport at 8:45 pm MDT and moved east and hit around 9 pm MDT on July 12th on interstate 15 southbound near the Northgate interchange.   It picked up a large Idaho Transportation Department portable digital sign and hurled in into traffic on Interstate 15 southbound.  The sign struck a car and a SUV heavily damaging both vehicles and both had to be towed from the interstate.   No injuries occurred to the drivers or passengers.  A pine tree snapped and fell in the parking lot of the National Weather Service office parking lot in Pocatello.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very large tree over 3 feet in diameter was uprooted and crashed into a residence on E 400 S just west of S 350 E.  Roof and the back half of residence were destroyed.  A second large tree next to the residence also uprooted.   Large trees were snapped off approximately half way up along E 400 S near S 250 E.  2 other large trees 2-3 feet in diameter uprooted half way in between S 350 E and S 250 E and power pole was snapped in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind shear and modest instability allowed for a severe storm to develop west of the town of Harpster.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter-sized hail was reported in the town of Harpster, Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved over southern portions of Grangeville, Idaho and areas along U.S. Highway 95 southwest of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved over southern portions of Grangeville, Idaho and areas along U.S. Highway 95 southwest of the city. Large amounts of hail created hazardous driving conditions, causing a vehicle to slide off the highway. Ping-pong-sized hail was reported along the highway, 5 miles southwest of Grangeville, with nickel-sized hail being reported in the city itself.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced large hail over southern portions of the Bitterroot National Forest on July 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spot Mountain Lookout in the Bitterroot National Forest reported quarter-size hail during the morning hours of July 3rd, as a line of thunderstorms moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Desert Ride Fire about 12 miles west of Rexburg began August 11th and was human caused. West winds of 15 to 20 mph caused the grass and brush fire to grow to 7,500 acres in the Menan Butte area and was caused by a man shooting a 22 caliber rifle at a firework on the ground causing it to ignite. The fire was contained on August 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Desert Ride Fire about 12 miles west of Rexburg began August 11th and was human caused. West winds of 15 to 20 mph caused the grass and brush fire to grow to 7,500 acres in the Menan Butte area and was caused by a man shooting a 22 caliber rifle at a firework on the ground causing it to ignite. The fire was contained on August 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was the coldest October on record in southeast Idaho.   Here is the list of locations having the coldest October.||American Falls|Ashton|Craters of the Moon|Dubois|Fort Hall|Hamer|Howe|Ketchum|McCammon|Tetonia|Burley|Idaho Falls|Pocatello|Stanley|Challis|Aberdeen|Blackfoot|Chilly Barton Flat|Downey|Grace|Island Park|Lifton|Mackay|Malad City|Minidoka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although the entire Pocatello forecast area experienced the all time coldest October on record will focus on the Lower Snake River Plain for a single event report.   Here are some of the highlights in the data recorded by the Pocatello ASOS located at the Pocatello Regional Airport:  ||The monthly average temperatures of 38.4 degrees was 4.1 degrees colder than the previous monthly low average of 42.5 degrees set in 2002.||10 record low temperatures were set during the month on the 3rd, 10th, 11th, 12th, 24th, 27th, 28th, 29th, 30th, and 31st.   ||The -6 degrees on October 30th was the first ever below zero temperature recorded in October in Pocatello and 13 degrees colder than the previous coldest October temperature recorded of 7 above.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly egg-sized hail with up to baseballs fell between Slate Creek and Riggins, Idaho and damaged greenhouse roofs, fences, pump house tanks, PVC pipelines, windshields, vehicles and orchards.

These elements promoted widespread afternoon and evening thunderstorms with heavy rain across north Idaho with scattered reports of large hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Benewah County Sheriff reported two trees blown down by thunderstorm winds near St. Maries. Trees were blocking a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An evening severe thunderstorm brought damaging hail and flash flooding along Highway 95 in central Idaho. Another storm brought quarter-sized hail and a debris flow north of Salmon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm put down an inch and a half of rain in 30 minutes over the canyons near Lucile. This caused large debris flows in Fiddle Creek, Sheep Creek and Chair Creek that covered US-95 with up to a 4 feet deep of mud. More than a dozen vehicles became stuck in the mud. The highway was closed until the next day when one lane was opened up. The highway opened up totally by Monday, three days later. The flooding also damaged Cow Creek Road and John Day Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 1,300 acre fire 3 miles northeast of Shoshone began by lightning on July 22nd and burned grass and brush was controlled by July 25th.   No structures were threatened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 1,300 acre fire 3 miles northeast of Shoshone began by lightning on July 22nd and burned grass and brush was controlled by July 25th.   No structures were threatened.  Highway 75 was shut down for a very short time on July 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle during the evening of December 2nd caused slick roads in the Idaho Falls region.

One collision occurred at the intersection of North Holmes and Hemmert Ave.  Two vehicles hit power poles.  Freezing drizzle reported in Ammon, Rigby, and Ucon as well.  Interstate 15 was reported icy from Shelley to Idaho Falls at 8 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on December 31st and lasting into the morning of January 1st, 2020 a moist northwesterly flow off the Pacific enhanced over a warm front into widespread snow over the Idaho Panhandle. Farther south over the Palouse region and Camas Prairie snow amounts were lighter and in the range of 1 to 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought significant snow and gusty winds to the higher terrain and backcountry of central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General snow amounts of 4 to 9 inches fell above 4000 feet with some locations above 6000 feet, recording over 12 inches of snow. Also, wind gusts to 40 mph were observed on the higher terrain. Difficult travel was experienced by backcountry users with periodic slippery, snow-covered, primary roads such as Highway 12 at Lolo Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of May 25th and into the morning of May 26th a strong north to south pressure gradient formed over north Idaho. While this gradient supported  general 15 to 25 mph sustained winds with gusts as high as 37 mph recorded by regional  observations, local terrain channeling of these winds produced a small area of uprooted large pine trees resulting in damage to a pair of cars and at least one building near the south end of Priest Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large trees were uprooted at the southern end of Priest lake by locally strong winds during the morning of May 26th. The trees crushed an SUV and damaged a pickup truck as well as heavily damaging a shed with minor roof damage to the adjacent house. This was a very localized event and probably the result of terrain channeling between nearby mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of May 30th an isolated thunderstorm formed just southeast of Moscow. Heavy rain from this storm caused a localized flash flood along an intermittent stream bed, which washed out a culvert and over topped it's banks to flow down a driveway and inundate the residential parking area with 6 inches of water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter submitted photos of a Flash Flood flowing down a driveway and washing out a culvert creating a breach in the driveway 3 to 4 feet deep with a similar width.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system situated off the coast of Oregon spread moisture into parts of Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho with areas of heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Cambridge reported 7 inches of new snow and trained spotters reported 4 to 5 inches around the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moved through the Pacific Northwest spreading areas of heavy snow across parts of Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Cambridge reported 6 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass overran valley cold air trapped across eastern Lemhi County,  leading to a prolonged period of heavy snowfall the evening December 31st into the morning of January 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall was reported across northern areas of eastern Lemhi County. Salmon was impacted by 8 inches of snowfall while areas along US 93 between Gibbonsville to Lost Trail Pass saw 12 inches. Blowing snow also caused significantly reduced visibility on Idaho Highway 28 between Leadore and Gilmore Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the evening of December 18th through the morning of December 20th a deep fetch of Pacific moisture was directed into the region. This moisture enhanced along a warm front and produced heavy snow across the mountains of north Idaho. In most of the valleys the warm front quickly raised snow levels limiting snow accumulations to 1 to 3 inches before changing over to rain, however the change over was slower in the far northern valleys where a number of reports indicated 4 to 6 inches of snow accumulated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Schweitzer Mountain Ski Resort reported 13 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of December 10th through the morning of December 12th a warm front and a following cold front passage delivered heavy snow accumulations to the mountains of north Idaho and the valleys of far north Idaho. Mountain accumulations ranged from 10 to 20 inches during this period while the northern most valleys near the Canadian border also received 4 to 10 inches of new snow accumulation. Amounts of 1 to 3 inches were noted in the Coeur D'Alene area and the valleys of the Central Panhandle Mountains. Melting snow in the wake of this storm triggered a small rock slide across highway 200 in north Idaho which was quickly cleared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Prichard reported 4.1 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

Melting snow in the wake of this storm triggered a small rock slide across highway 200 in north Idaho which was quickly cleared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 10 miles north of Sandpoint recorded 6.4 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure system over central Oregon produced a very strong northeasterly pressure gradient across eastern Washington and north Idaho on November 27th. Mesonet weather stations reported peak wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range, however terrain constriction of winds funneling down the Purcell Trench and adjacent valleys of north Idaho produced locally higher gusts which downed numerous trees and caused multiple power outages.  An estimated 2000 residents of eastern Washington and north Idaho lost power during this wind storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bonner County Emergency Manager reported large trees blown down damaging a home southeast of Priest River on Dufort Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of Pacific moisture brought heavy snow mainly to the mountains of central and southeast Idaho with many SNOTEL sites exceeding 2 feet of snow accumulation.  Many roads closed in the 4 day period and many schools were also forced to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multi day storm brought heavy snow and wind to the Raft River region.   By the afternoon of the 13th, 5 miles northwest of Holbrook, 8 inches of snow had already been recorded.  More snow likely fell in the data sparse region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended atmospheric river event occurred for an entire week.   Extremely heavy snow fell in the mountains with amounts at SNOTEL sites reaching as high as 5 to 8 feet for the week.  Many roads closed through the mountains.   Schools closed and some sheet flooding began mainly in Bingham and Minidoka counties where temperatures warmed significantly.  Avalanches also occurred in the central mountains with the heavy snowfall accompanied with warmer temperatures.  The storm wrapped up a month of record snowfall in the Upper Snake Highlands where the Island Park COOP observer recorded over 100 inches for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and wind caused the closure of US Highway 20 from Carey to Arco and also from Idaho Falls to the US Highway 26 intersection.   Snow amounts were not significant and the roads were opened later on in the storm after the closures from the 24th through the 25th.   The West Jefferson Schools were closed on the 25th due to the hazardous travel conditions.

Overall damage was not significant with the flooding.

Avalanches also occurred in the central mountains with the heavy snowfall accompanied with warmer temperatures.  The storm wrapped up a month of record snowfall in the Upper Snake Highlands where the Island Park COOP observer recorded over 100 inches for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell for several days in Sun Valley and the surrounding mountains.   An avalanche south of Galena Summit closed highway 75 for several hours on the 26th.   The COOP observer at the Ketchum Ranger Station recorded 26 inches of snow with the storm.   SNOTEL amounts were:  Chocolate Gulch 23 inches, Dollarhide Summit 50 inches, Galena 40 inches, Galena Summit 35 inches, Hyndman 41 inches, and Lost Wood Divide 65 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From September 8th through September 11th a slow moving upper level low pressure with a shield of wrap around Pacific moisture passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho.  Areas of moderate to locally heavy rain showers and a few thunderstorms ranged through out the region during this cool and unstable wet period. One of these storms produced a short lived funnel cloud near Rathdrum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief Funnel Cloud was reported by the public to local broadcast media near Rathdrum. Time estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 28th and 29th a cold and deep upper level low descended into the region from Canada with a band of wrap-around moisture feeding into this storm system. During the late afternoon and into the night of September 28th precipitation from this system fell as early season accumulating snow in the mountains and the valleys of north Idaho. In addition gusty north winds funneling down the Purcell Trench valley of north Idaho produced local wind gusts to 45 mph.  Heavy wet snow along with gusty winds caused broken tree limbs and in some cases toppled trees which had not shed their leaves.||In the Coeur D'Alene area scattered power outages occurred and moderate snow accumulations impacted traffic during the evening commute period. The mountains of the Northern Panhandle zone received early season heavy snow while the valleys received generally moderate amounts but with locally heavy accumulations. Numerous power outages were noted in this cooler and windier zone during this storm with at one point over 1200 customers without power in Bonner County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 6 miles south of Coeur D'Alene recorded 2.9 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 28th and 29th a cold and deep upper level low descended into the region from Canada with a band of wrap-around moisture feeding into this storm system. During the late afternoon and into the night of September 28th precipitation from this system fell as early season accumulating snow in the mountains and the valleys of north Idaho. In addition gusty north winds funneling down the Purcell Trench valley of north Idaho produced local wind gusts to 45 mph.  Heavy wet snow along with gusty winds caused broken tree limbs and in some cases toppled trees which had not shed their leaves.||In the Coeur D'Alene area scattered power outages occurred and moderate snow accumulations impacted traffic during the evening commute period. The mountains of the Northern Panhandle zone received early season heavy snow while the valleys received generally moderate amounts but with locally heavy accumulations. Numerous power outages were noted in this cooler and windier zone during this storm with at one point over 1200 customers without power in Bonner County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Athol reported 3 inches of overnight new snow accumulation.
